---
title: "Day 39 — Post-training: how a next-token predictor becomes an assistant"
subtitle: "Monday, 31 August 2026 · Week 6 · Scaling and post-training · Study day · 3-4 h"
---

::: {.day-nav .kind-study}
[← Day 38](day-38.qmd) · [All 50 days](../timeline.qmd) · [Day 40 →](day-40.qmd)
:::

::: {.why}
A pretrained model completes text; it does not answer you. Jurafsky and Martin lay out the whole post-training pipeline in 18 calm pages, so you meet SFT, reward models and DPO once as textbook material today and once as an argumentative paper tomorrow.
:::

## What you should be able to do tonight

- Explain, without notes, what instruction tuning changes about a model and what it leaves untouched.
- Write the Bradley-Terry preference model from 9.2.2 and say why it is the same paired-comparison likelihood you would fit for ranked outcomes anywhere else.
- State the difference between PPO (9.3.1) and DPO (9.3.2) in terms of what each one actually trains.

## Tasks <span class='task-total'>190 min</span>

::: {.task-list}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [45 min]{.task-min} Read 9.1 Instruction Tuning, including 9.1.1 on where instruction data comes from and 9.1.2 on why honest evaluation of an instruction-tuned model requires leaving out whole task clusters rather than held-out examples within a task.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [45 min]{.task-min} Read 9.2 Learning from Preferences carefully - 9.2.1 on preference data, 9.2.2 on modelling preferences, 9.2.3 on learning to score them. Derive the pairwise loss -log sigmoid(r(x, y_w) - r(x, y_l)) from the Bradley-Terry model yourself before checking it against the chapter.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [40 min]{.task-min} Read 9.3 (PPO, DPO, evaluation, and 9.3.4 on what preference-based learning cannot fix) and 9.4 on test-time compute, including 9.4.1 on chain-of-thought prompting.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [60 min]{.task-min} Take 20 cases from your Day 35 failure taxonomy - the ones your Day 33 prompt got wrong - and rerun them with a chain-of-thought prompt in the style of 9.4.1. Record how many flip to correct, how many flip the other way, and the added latency per generation.
:::
:::

## Resources

- [SLP3 Ch9 - Post-training: Instruction Tuning, Alignment, Test-Time Compute](https://web.stanford.edu/~jurafsky/slp3/9.pdf) [textbook]{.res-kind}

## Deliverable

::: {.deliverable}
A one-page diagram of pretraining to SFT to preference data to reward model to policy optimization, annotated with what data each arrow needs and who produces it, plus your chain-of-thought results on the 20 failure cases with flips counted in both directions and the latency cost.
:::

## Checkpoint

::: {.checkpoint}
Preference learning needs pairs. Where do the two candidate responses in a pair come from, and why does it matter whether they come from the model you are about to train or from a different one?
:::

## Prepare for tomorrow

Ten minutes tonight so Day 40, Tuesday, 1 September 2026 starts clean. This is what the evening email sends you.

::: {.task-list .prep}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Download InstructGPT (arXiv:2203.02155). It is long, but you only read Sections 1, 3, 4 and 5.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Write down your guess at how many people OpenAI hired to produce the demonstration and preference data. You check it tomorrow.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Flag which of today's chain-of-thought flips went the wrong way - those cases come back tomorrow when you think about who would label clinical preference data.
:::
:::
